Factor Analysis

Author

Riccardo Volpato

Data Processing

library(tidyverse)
library(gtools)
library(psych)
library(knitr)

set.seed(8675309) # makes sure random numbers are reproducible
source("read_qualtrics_data.R")
d <- load_data("data/pilot/TPSai_17+March+2026_16.59.csv")
cols_tpsai <- d$cols_tpsai

# switch to d$items_data after data collection
efa_data <- read_csv("data/pilot/simulated_efa_data.csv") %>%
  select(all_of(cols_tpsai))

Analysis Structure

Before proceeding to our factor analysis we need to make two key decisions about its structure: 1. Whether we exclude any items from factor analysis 2. How to group our sub-scales

Item Exclusion

Al thought we have already covered this step in our item reduction analysis, we re-examine skewness and kurtosis for each item in our new data sample to check whether responses are spread across the scale or piled up at one end. Items where most people gave the same answer have little variation, which makes it harder for factor analysis to detect the underlying structure, regardless of the estimation method used.

Following Kline (2016), we treat absolute skewness and kurtosis values above 1.5 as a sign that an item’s responses are too bunched together to be useful. Items exceeding this threshold will be considered for removal alongside the other item reduction criteria. Additionally, we may consider keeping items with high skew or kurtosis for comparison or reporting purposes but flag them for removal from factor analysis.

dist_threshold <- 1.5

dist_data <- efa_data %>%
  summarise(
    across(all_of(everything()), list(
        skew = ~ psych::skew(.x,    na.rm = TRUE),
        kurt = ~ psych::kurtosi(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
      ), .names = "{.col}_{.fn}")
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to  = c("item", ".value"),
    names_sep = "_(?=[^_]+$)"
  ) %>%
  separate(item, c("subscale", "q_n"), sep = "_", remove = FALSE)

ggplot(dist_data, aes(x = skew, y = kurt, colour = subscale, label = item)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-dist_threshold, dist_threshold), linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-dist_threshold, dist_threshold), linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Skewness", y = "Kurtosis")
dist_data %>%
  filter(abs(skew) > dist_threshold | abs(kurt) > dist_threshold) %>% 
  select(subscale, item, skew, kurt) %>% 
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
subscale item skew kurt

Sub-scale Grouping

We consider three options for factor analysis: 1. Conduct separate factor analysis for each sub-scale 2. Conduct a single factor analysis across sub-scales 3. Conduct a factor analysis for trust and distrust items, merging Trust and Corporate Distrust into a single sub-scale

Before empirical examination, some theoretical argument worth considering are: * the theoretical independence of Trust and Distrust as related but distinct constructs warrants separate investigation (McKnight and Chervany 2001; Scharowski et al. 2024), suggesting either option 1 or 3. * combining all items in a single analysis would introduce a mixed-valence structure. Trust items are positively worded while Distrust and Corporate Distrust items are negatively worded, which risks producing factors driven by wording artefacts rather than substantive constructs (Zhang, Noor, and Savalei 2016), suggesting either option 1 or 3. * Corporate Distrust is conceptually distinct from Distrust as it targets an organisational entity rather than an AI system directly, suggesting option 1 over option 3, although weakly because it could be a factor. * Trust and Distrust (including Corporate Distrust) items are more or less of equal length (22 vs 21) suggesting two lenght-balanced sub-scales, suggesting option 3 over 1.

Correlations

First, we report Spearman correlations between the three sub-scale mean scores (Trust, Distrust, and Corporate Distrust) to examine their inter-relationships prior to factor analysis. Spearman correlations are used as a rank-based, distribution-free alternative to Pearson, which is more appropriate for composite scores derived from ordinal Likert items (Boateng et al. 2018).

If any two subscales correlate above .80, indicating substantial construct overlap, we consider combining them for factor analysis into either option 2 or 3.

subscales <- efa_data %>%
  mutate(
    Trust    = rowMeans(pick(all_of(d$cols_trust)),    na.rm = TRUE),
    Distrust = rowMeans(pick(all_of(d$cols_distrust)), na.rm = TRUE),
    "Corporate Distrust"  = rowMeans(pick(all_of(d$cols_company)),  na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  select(Trust, Distrust, "Corporate Distrust")


cor_subscales <- cor(subscales, method = "spearman", use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

cor_subscales %>% 
  as.data.frame() %>% 
  rownames_to_column("S1") %>% 
  pivot_longer(-S1, names_to = "S2", values_to = "r") %>% 
  filter(S1 < S2) %>% 
  kable(digits = 2)
S1 S2 r
Distrust Trust -0.49
Corporate Distrust Trust -0.43
Corporate Distrust Distrust 0.40

Factorability

We examing two factorability checks for different sub-scale groupings.

The Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) index asks whether the correlations among items are sufficiently compact and patterned to yield reliable factors. Values above .80 indicate good sampling adequacy and values above .90 are excellent. We treat KMO as our primary indicator of factorability.

Bartlett’s test of sphericity asks a simpler question: is the correlation matrix significantly different from an identity matrix, where all items would be completely uncorrelated? A significant result (p < .05) confirms there is at least some correlation structure for factor analysis to work with. However, Bartlett’s test is sensitive to sample size and tends to be significant even when correlations are trivially small, so we report it for completeness rather than as a primary decision criterion.

Both tests are computed on the polychoric correlation matrix rather than the standard Pearson correlation matrix, as polychoric correlations are more appropriate for ordinal Likert data (Boateng et al. 2018). A separate matrix is computed for each subscale, since we are conducting independent EFAs for Trust, Distrust, and Corporate Distrust rather than analysing all items together.

factorability <- function(R, n) {
  tibble(
    kmo  = KMO(R)$MSA,
    chi2 = cortest.bartlett(R, n)$chisq,
    df   = cortest.bartlett(R, n)$df,
    p    = cortest.bartlett(R, n)$p.value
  )
}

R_all        <- polychoric(efa_data %>% select(all_of(d$cols_tpsai)))$rho
R_trust      <- polychoric(efa_data %>% select(all_of(d$cols_trust)))$rho
R_distrust_1 <- polychoric(efa_data %>% select(all_of(d$cols_distrust)))$rho
R_distrust_2 <- polychoric(efa_data %>% select(all_of(c(d$cols_distrust, d$cols_company))))$rho
R_company    <- polychoric(efa_data %>% select(all_of(d$cols_company)))$rho

bind_rows(
  factorability(R_all,    nrow(efa_data)),
  factorability(R_trust,    nrow(efa_data)),
  factorability(R_distrust_1, nrow(efa_data)),
  factorability(R_company,  nrow(efa_data)),
  factorability(R_distrust_2, nrow(efa_data)),
) %>%
  mutate(subscale = c("All", "Trust", "Distrust Single", "Corporate Distrust", "Distrust (including Corporate)")) %>%
  select(subscale, everything()) %>%
  kable(digits = 3,
        col.names = c("Subscale", "KMO", "χ²", "df", "p"))
Subscale KMO χ² df p
All 0.232 21036.224 903 0
Trust 0.788 5688.885 231 0
Distrust Single 0.775 2759.756 91 0
Corporate Distrust 0.558 697.114 21 0
Distrust (including Corporate) 0.826 5114.528 210 0

Exploratory Factor Analysis

Exploratory factor analysis (EFA) is a statistical technique for identifying hidden structure in a set of items. The core idea is simple: if many items correlate with each other, they are probably all measuring some common underlying thing, a latent factor. EFA works by finding the smallest number of latent factors that can reproduce the observed correlations among items. It does this by asking: if these factors exist, what pattern of correlations between items would they produce? The solution that best matches the actual observed correlations, leaving the smallest residuals, is retained. Because EFA makes no assumptions about which items belong to which factor, it is well suited to the early stages of scale development, where the goal is to let the data reveal the underlying structure rather than confirm a predetermined one.

Parallel Analysis

A key decision in EFA is how many factors to extract. Extracting too few factors leaves meaningful structure unexplained; extracting too many produces factors that are essentially fitting noise in the data.

Parallel analysis addresses this by comparing each factor’s eigenvalue from the real correlation matrix to the corresponding eigenvalue from random correlation matrices of the same dimensions. Each factor is associated with an eigenvalue. Mathematically, an eigenvalue summarises how much of the shared variance among items a factor captures. When EFA extracts a factor, it finds a weighted combination of items that explains as much of the common variance as possible, the eigenvalue is the sum of the squared factor loadings for that factor across all items. A large eigenvalue therefore means many items have high loadings on that factor. Eigenvalues are computed using MINRES estimation, which minimises the residual correlations between the observed and reproduced correlation matrices and makes no distributional assumptions. In parallel analysis, factors are ordered by their eigenvalues from largest to smallest, with the first eigenvalue belonging to the first factor, the one explaining the most variance of the real correlation matrix, the second to the second factor, and so on. For each factor in turn, if the eigenvalue from the real data exceeds the average eigenvalue from the random matrices, meaning the factor is accounting for more variance than a factor extracted from pure noise would, that factor is considered to reflect genuine underlying structure in the data and is worth retaining. The moment the real eigenvalue drops below the random one, which is also what the scree plot visualises, with the blue line (real data) dropping below the red line (random data), that factor and all subsequent ones are considered noise and discarded.

fa_trust <- fa.parallel(R_trust,    n.obs = nrow(efa_data), 
            fm = "minres", fa = "fa",
            main = "Parallel Analysis — Trust")
Parallel analysis suggests that the number of factors =  5  and the number of components =  NA 
fa_distrust <- fa.parallel(R_distrust_2, n.obs = nrow(efa_data), 
            fm = "minres", fa = "fa",
            main = "Parallel Analysis — Distrust")
Parallel analysis suggests that the number of factors =  5  and the number of components =  NA

Factor Loadings

Once the number of factors is determined, we extract them using MINRES estimation with oblimin rotation.

MINRES (minimum residual) estimation finds the factor solution that minimises the residuals, the differences between the observed correlation matrix and the one reproduced by the factor model. It makes no distributional assumptions, making it well suited to ordinal Likert data compared to maximum likelihood estimation, which assumes multivariate normality. It is the same method we used to compute eigenvalues and determine the number of factors.

Rotation is applied after initial factor extraction to make the solution more interpretable. Without rotation, factors are mathematically defined but often difficult to interpret because most items load moderately onto most factors. Rotation redistributes the variance among factors to achieve a simpler, cleaner pattern where each item loads strongly onto one factor and weakly onto others (Buchanan, Valentine, and Schulenberg 2014).

We use oblimin, which is an oblique rotation method meaning factors are allowed to correlate with one another. This is theoretically appropriate here because items within each subscale are expected to measure related but distinct aspects of the same broader construct:. An orthogonal rotation such as varimax would force factors to be uncorrelated, which is a stronger and less realistic assumption in psychological measurement (Boateng et al. 2018). Importantly, if the factors turn out to be empirically uncorrelated, oblimin will recover the same solution as an orthogonal rotation, so nothing is lost by choosing oblique rotation.

efa_trust    <- fa(R_trust,
                   nfactors = fa_trust$nfact,
                   n.obs = nrow(efa_data),
                   fm = "minres", rotate = "oblimin")

efa_distrust <- fa(R_distrust_2,
                   nfactors = fa_distrust$nfact,
                   n.obs = nrow(efa_data),
                   fm = "minres", rotate = "oblimin")

Below we display factor loadings for the different EFA that we ran. We are looking for a factor structure that is robust and simple, that is loadings have high values in one factor only (Buchanan, Valentine, and Schulenberg 2014). More specifically we defined high loadings as loadings above 0.6 and high values in only one factor when the difference between the highest loading and the second highest is above 0.2.

get_loadings <- function(efa, primary_threshold = 0.6, cross_load_diff = 0.2) {
  m <- efa$loadings %>% unclass() %>% as.data.frame() %>% round(2)
  factor_cols <- names(m)
  
  m %>%
    rownames_to_column("Item") %>%
    mutate(
      max        = apply(across(all_of(factor_cols)), 1, function(x) max(abs(x), na.rm = TRUE)) %>% round(2),
      n_loadings = apply(across(all_of(factor_cols)), 1, function(x) sum(abs(x) >= 0.30, na.rm = TRUE)),
      top2_diff  = apply(across(all_of(factor_cols)), 1, function(x) {
        sorted <- sort(abs(x), decreasing = TRUE)
        if (length(sorted) >= 2) round(sorted[1] - sorted[2], 2) else NA
      }),
      flag = case_when(
        max < primary_threshold              ~ "weak primary",
        top2_diff < cross_load_diff          ~ "cross-load",
        TRUE                                 ~ ""
      )
    ) %>%
    select(-top2_diff) %>%
    mutate(across(all_of(factor_cols), ~ ifelse(abs(.x) < 0.30, "—", .x)))
}

efa_trust_loads <- get_loadings(efa_trust)
efa_distrust_loads <- get_loadings(efa_distrust)

efa_trust_loads %>% kable()

Analysis Decisions

We can judge whether the resulting factor structure of our analysis is not robust nor simple through a few heuristics: * more than >20% of items have been flagged * any one factor has less 3 un-flagged items * cross-loadings do not have theoretically plausible explanations

In these cases, we can iterate on our factor solutions through two options: 1. Reduce the number of factors 2. Drop contentious items

Reducing the number of factors

Two considerations for reducing the number of factors: a) re-examining the scree-plot of the parallel analysis and inspect whethere there is a clear elbow in the plot before the actual data drops below the random one, suggesting that this could be a more suitable number of factors.

  1. examining the factor correlation matrix: if some factors are highly correlated (say r > 0.6), that’s a sign they might be better represented as a single factor.
efa_trust$Phi %>% 
  as.data.frame() %>%
  round(2) %>%
  kable()
MR1 MR2 MR3 MR5 MR4
MR1 1.00 0.17 -0.05 0.10 0.00
MR2 0.17 1.00 -0.03 -0.25 0.03
MR3 -0.05 -0.03 1.00 -0.05 0.12
MR5 0.10 -0.25 -0.05 1.00 0.03
MR4 0.00 0.03 0.12 0.03 1.00 
efa_distrust$Phi %>% 
  as.data.frame() %>%
  round(2) %>%
  kable()
MR1 MR2 MR3 MR4 MR5
MR1 1.00 -0.04 -0.06 0.02 0.13
MR2 -0.04 1.00 -0.07 0.10 0.24
MR3 -0.06 -0.07 1.00 0.09 0.03
MR4 0.02 0.10 0.09 1.00 0.00
MR5 0.13 0.24 0.03 0.00 1.00

Dropping items

To consider which items to drop, we can examing the items that we flagged when extracting factor loadings, either because they had a weak primary loading or because they cross-loaded

efa_trust_loads %>% 
  filter(flag != "") %>% 
  kable()
Item MR1 MR2 MR3 MR5 MR4 max n_loadings flag
TR_02 0.64 — -0.45 — — 0.64 2 cross-load
TR_05 — -0.62 — 0.53 — 0.62 2 cross-load
TR_07 -0.4 -0.49 — 0.5 — 0.50 3 weak primary
TR_08 — — 0.57 0.51 0.41 0.57 3 weak primary
TR_12 0.62 — — — -0.58 0.62 2 cross-load
TR_19 0.3 — -0.46 0.57 — 0.57 3 weak primary 
efa_distrust_loads %>% 
  filter(flag != "") %>% 
  kable()
Item MR1 MR2 MR3 MR4 MR5 max n_loadings flag
DT_04 0.33 0.5 0.35 — 0.45 0.50 4 weak primary
DT_10 — — 0.53 0.62 — 0.62 2 cross-load
CD_01 — 0.36 — -0.46 0.54 0.54 3 weak primary
CD_04 0.4 -0.42 0.32 -0.37 0.58 0.58 5 weak primary

Some heuristic considerations for dropping flagged items: * weak primary loading (max < 0.50) migth mean that the item isn’t contributing much anywhere * small cross-load differences (< 0.1) across multiple factors indicates that the item is highly ambigious * we can also examine item-total correlation within its primary loading factor (below). The ambiguous items might have low correlations with the items that cleanly define each factor, which would confirm they don’t really belong anywhere.

item_total_by_factor <- function(efa, data) {
  efa$loadings %>% unclass() %>% as.data.frame() %>%
    rownames_to_column("item") %>%
    pivot_longer(-item, names_to = "factor", values_to = "loading") %>%
    filter(abs(loading) >= 0.30) %>%
    group_by(item) %>%
    slice_max(abs(loading), n = 1) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    group_by(factor) %>%
    group_map(~ {
      data %>% select(all_of(.x$item)) %>%
        psych::alpha() %>% .$item.stats %>%
        rownames_to_column("item") %>% select(item, r = r.drop)
    }, .keep = TRUE) %>%
    bind_rows() %>%
    kable()
}

item_total_by_factor(efa_trust, efa_data)
Some items ( TR_05 ) were negatively correlated with the first principal component and 
probably should be reversed.  
To do this, run the function again with the 'check.keys=TRUE' option

References

Boateng, Godfred O., Torsten B. Neilands, Edward A. Frongillo, Hugo R. Melgar-Quiñonez, and Sera L. Young. 2018. “Best Practices for Developing and Validating Scales for Health, Social, and Behavioral Research: A Primer.” Frontiers in Public Health 6 (June). https://doi.org/10.3389/fpubh.2018.00149.
Buchanan, Erin M., Kathrene D. Valentine, and Stefan E. Schulenberg. 2014. “Exploratory and Confirmatory Factor Analysis: Developing the Purpose in Life Testshort Form.” SAGE Research Methods Cases, January. https://doi.org/10.1007/S10902-008-9124-3.
Kline, Rex B. 2016. Principles and Practice of Structural Equation Modeling, 4th Ed. Principles and Practice of Structural Equation Modeling, 4th Ed. New York, NY, US: The Guilford Press.
McKnight, D. Harrison, and Norman L. Chervany. 2001. “Trust and Distrust Definitions: One Bite at a Time.” In, edited by Rino Falcone, Munindar Singh, and Yao-Hua Tan, 2246:27–54. Berlin, Heidelberg: Springer Berlin Heidelberg. https://doi.org/10.1007/3-540-45547-7_3.
Scharowski, Nicolas, Sebastian A. C. Perrig, Lena Fanya Aeschbach, Nick von Felten, Klaus Opwis, Philipp Wintersberger, and Florian Brühlmann. 2024. “To Trust or Distrust Trust Measures: Validating Questionnaires for Trust in AI.” arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2403.00582.
Zhang, Xijuan, Ramsha Noor, and Victoria Savalei. 2016. “Examining the Effect of Reverse Worded Items on the Factor Structure of the Need for Cognition Scale.” PLoS ONE 11 (6): e0157795. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0157795.
